metadata.summary: Five named decomposition patterns (Extract Service, Component, Module, Configuration, Types) with size triggers, criteria, and language-specific layouts for Python, TypeScript, Go.

content.title: Code Decomposition Patterns
content.summary: Fat files (controllers >100 lines, React components >150 lines, settings >150 lines) reduce testability, increase merge conflicts, and obscure responsibility. Named patterns give agents and reviewers a shared vocabulary and precise post-conditions: view files stay thin, services are framework-free, components compose, config splits by environment.

why.p: Fat files (controllers >100 lines, React components >150 lines, settings >150 lines) reduce testability, increase merge conflicts, and obscure responsibility. Named patterns give agents and reviewers a shared vocabulary and precise post-conditions: view files stay thin, services are framework-free, components compose, config splits by environment.

list.item: Controller/view exceeds 100 lines with mixed HTTP handling and business logic → Extract Service.
React/Vue component exceeds 150 lines with multiple UI concerns → Extract Component.
Flat directory exceeds 20 files or team ownership is unclear → Extract Module.
Settings file exceeds 150 lines or mixes environment-specific config → Extract Configuration.
TypeScript files mix types with logic or types are reused across multiple files → Extract Types.
New feature scaffolding where the agent must lay out files before writing code.
Features totalling ≤200 lines — applying a pattern creates more files than logic.
Framework-mandated single files (e.g., Django `urls.py`) — forced coupling is not a decomposition target.
Generated code or ORM migrations — structural changes break the generator contract.
Performance-critical hot loops where indirection has a measured cost.

section.name: five-named-decomposition-patterns
section.p: Catalog of Extract Service, Extract Component, Extract Module, Extract Configuration, and Extract Types with triggers and acceptance criteria.

rule.statement: Move business logic from controllers/views into a dedicated service class when the controller exceeds 100 lines or mixes HTTP handling with domain logic.
rule.rationale: Services are testable without the HTTP layer and reusable across endpoints. Thin controllers become a translation layer only: deserialize input, call service, serialize output.

rule.statement: Service methods must not accept framework-typed parameters (request, response, HttpContext). Accept plain domain types only.
rule.rationale: Framework types in service signatures create coupling that defeats the purpose of extraction — the service becomes untestable without a full HTTP stack.

rule.statement: Split a React/Vue component that exceeds 150 lines into a parent composition component (<50 lines) and focused child components (<100 lines each).
rule.rationale: Single-responsibility components are easier to test, memoize, and reuse. Extract when multiple UI concerns coexist or when the component is difficult to test in isolation.

rule.statement: Extract data-fetching hooks to separate files (e.g., useDashboardData.ts); keep components presentation-only.
rule.rationale: Separating data and presentation enables testing each concern independently and enables re-use of the hook across multiple views.

rule.statement: Reorganize a flat directory with 20+ files into domain-based subdirectories when team ownership is unclear or related files are scattered.
rule.rationale: Domain modules create explicit boundaries, reduce cognitive load for new contributors, and enable independent deployment in the future.

rule.statement: Split a settings file exceeding 150 lines into base.py + per-environment overrides (development.py, production.py, testing.py) and per-concern components (database.py, cache.py, security.py).
rule.rationale: Monolithic settings files make environment-specific overrides fragile and security settings hard to audit. Splitting makes each concern independently readable.

rule.statement: __init__.py must determine which env file to import; env files must never import from each other (prevents circular imports).
rule.rationale: Circular imports in config cause import errors that are hard to diagnose. One-way load from __init__.py is the only safe pattern.

rule.statement: Move TypeScript type/interface definitions to dedicated types/ files when types are reused across multiple files or when circular type dependencies appear.
rule.rationale: Centralized types create a single source of truth, make refactoring safer, and improve IDE go-to-definition accuracy.

section.name: language-specific-directory-layouts
section.p: Reference directory trees for Python/Django, TypeScript/React, and Go after applying decomposition patterns.

example.title: Python/Django layout
example.code: app/ ├── models/ │ ├── __init__.py # Export all models │ ├── user.py # User model only │ └── profile.py # Profile model only ├── services/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── user_service.py # User business logic (no HTTP types) │ └── auth_service.py # Auth business logic ├── views/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── user_views.py # Thin: deserialize -> service -> serialize │ └── auth_views.py ├── serializers/ │ ├── user_serializers.py │ └── auth_serializers.py └── tests/ ├── test_models/ ├── test_services/ # Test services without HTTP layer └── test_views/
example.note: Services imported by views; views import nothing from tests. Circular imports are structurally impossible.

example.title: TypeScript/React layout
example.code: src/ ├── components/ │ ├── ui/ # shadcn primitives (Button, Card, etc.) │ └── features/ │ └── Dashboard/ │ ├── Dashboard.tsx # Composition only (<50 lines) │ ├── DashboardHeader.tsx │ ├── DashboardMetrics.tsx │ └── hooks/ │ └── useDashboardData.ts ├── hooks/ # Shared hooks ├── services/ # API service functions ├── stores/ # State management ├── types/ # Interface definitions (Extract Types) └── utils/ # Pure utility functions
example.note: Parent Dashboard.tsx renders children only; data fetching lives in hooks/. Each child <100 lines.

example.title: Go layout
example.code: internal/ ├── domain/ │ ├── user/ │ │ ├── user.go # Entity + value objects │ │ ├── repository.go # Interface (no implementation) │ │ └── service.go # Business logic │ └── order/ │ ├── order.go │ ├── repository.go │ └── service.go ├── infrastructure/ │ ├── database/ │ │ ├── postgres.go # Implements domain repository interfaces │ │ └── migrations/ │ └── http/ │ ├── server.go │ └── handlers/ # Thin handlers calling domain services └── application/ ├── commands/ └── queries/
example.note: Domain packages depend on nothing in infrastructure; infrastructure depends on domain interfaces. Dependency arrow points inward.

section.name: before-after-examples
section.p: Concrete code examples showing fat-to-thin transformations for the two most common patterns.

example.title: Fat controller (800 lines)
example.code: # views.py (800 lines) - fat controller class ShopView: def checkout(self, request): # validation (30 lines) # inventory check (20 lines) # payment processing (40 lines) # order creation (25 lines) # email notification (15 lines) # analytics tracking (10 lines) pass
example.note: Single method with 6 responsibilities; untestable without HTTP stack; impossible to reuse logic.

example.title: Extracted service
example.note: CheckoutService accepts plain dict (no HTTP types), fully testable in unit tests without HTTP stack.

example.title: Fat component (500 lines)
example.note: Data fetching, event handling, and rendering mixed. Impossible to test or memoize each concern separately.

example.title: Extracted components
example.note: Parent is a composition contract; each child tests independently; hook is reusable across multiple pages.

section.name: decomposition-antipatterns
section.p: Common failures when agents apply decomposition patterns: leaky abstractions, over-extraction, missed import updates, and barrel re-exports.

antipattern.description: Leaky service: extracted service method accepts request/response/HttpContext parameters.
antipattern.reason: Framework types in service signatures tie the service to the HTTP layer, defeating testability. Services must accept only plain domain types.

antipattern.description: Over-extraction: agent creates a micro-service for a 5-line helper with a single caller.
antipattern.reason: Unnecessary files increase cognitive overhead without adding testability or reusability. Apply a minimum threshold: a service/component must be >=40 lines or have >=3 callers to justify extraction.

antipattern.description: Import update misses: agent moves a file but only updates the moved file, not its importers.
antipattern.reason: Broken imports cause runtime errors that only appear when the code path is exercised. Always run a grep pass on importers + `tsc --noEmit` or `python -c 'import app'` after each move.

antipattern.description: Barrel re-export sprawl: agent adds `export *` in index.ts to make imports convenient after Extract Module.
antipattern.reason: `export *` re-exports everything including internals, breaks tree-shaking, creates implicit public APIs, and causes circular dependency risks. Forbid `export *`; export named symbols only.

antipattern.description: Settings circular import: env file (production.py) imports from base.py which imports from production.py.
antipattern.reason: Circular imports in config fail at import time with an inscrutable error. Enforce one-way load: __init__.py imports env file; env file imports base; base imports nothing from the settings package.

antipattern.description: Doc drift: pattern application without updating the directory's AGENTS.md in the same PR.
antipattern.reason: AGENTS.md file tables go stale immediately after a structural change. Always update AGENTS.md in the same commit as the decomposition.

template.purpose: CI script enforcing line-count invariants and detecting circular imports.
